title: Sewn booklet refill for a refillable journal
subtitle: Making a pamphlet-stitched insert that fits inside a refillable leather or fabric cover
excerpt: A refillable journal cover needs an insert that fits its elastic retainers or cord loops and lies flat when open. A pamphlet-stitched booklet is the simplest sewn structure that meets these requirements: three to five signatures, sewn together, trimmed square, and sized to slip into the cover retainer with 2 mm of play on each side.
sourceNotes: The pamphlet-stitch structure is documented in Douglas Cockerell, Bookbinding and the Care of Books (1901, public domain). The application to refillable journal inserts is a contemporary craft adaptation synthesised from modern traveller's notebook practice.

glossaryTerms.definition: A group of sheets folded together as a single unit for sewing. A four-sheet signature produces eight leaves or sixteen pages.
A simple three-hole or five-hole sewing pattern that holds a single signature or a group of signatures together along the spine fold.

content.text: A traveller's notebook insert is typically an A5, B6, or A6
signature
booklet, sewn along the fold with a
pamphlet stitch
and finished without a separate cover. The cover of the insert is a slightly heavier-weight sheet (150 to 220 gsm) folded around the signatures.
Sharp blades are safer than blunt ones. Keep the non-cutting hand behind the blade path when trimming the text block.
What you need
Measure the inside of the cover retainer (the elastic or cord loop that holds the insert). Cut the insert 2 mm narrower on each side to allow it to slide in and out without forcing.

Text pages: cartridge paper, 90 gsm, grain running parallel to the spine (short grain for a portrait insert). Fold into signatures of four sheets each. Three signatures gives 48 pages; five gives 80 pages.
Cover: one sheet of 180 gsm cartridge or coloured card, folded to wrap the signatures.
Folding and piercing the signatures
Fold each signature sheet in half and bone-fold the crease firmly.
Nest all signatures inside the cover sheet.
Pierce five sewing holes along the spine fold: one at the head, one at the tail, and three evenly spaced in between. Use the bookbinding awl from inside the fold outward.
Sewing the pamphlet stitch
Thread the needle with a length of waxed linen thread equal to three times the spine height.
Enter through hole 3 (the centre hole) from outside the cover, leaving a 50 mm tail.

Exit through hole 1 (the head). Re-enter through hole 2. Exit through hole 3. Cross the long thread (not the tail). Enter through hole 4. Exit through hole 5. Re-enter through hole 4. Exit through hole 3.
Tie off with the original tail thread. The knot should sit inside the booklet at hole 3.
Finishing
Press the finished insert flat under a weight for 15 minutes. Trim the fore-edge if any pages protrude unevenly. Slide the insert into the retainer: it should sit snugly without buckling.
